title: Алфавит и фонетика — Alphabet & Phonetics
description: The 33 letters of the Cyrillic alphabet, the sounds of Russian, stress, vowel reduction and consonant assimilation. Click any Russian text to hear it.

units.title: 1. The Cyrillic alphabet
units.summary: Russian uses 33 Cyrillic letters: 10 vowels, 21 consonants, and 2 signs that carry no sound of their own.

blocks.html: Russian is written in the Cyrillic script. Several letters look like Latin ones but sound different — these "false friends" (В, Е, Н, Р, С, У, Х) are the first hurdle. Master the alphabet first: everything else (grammar tables, dictionaries, stress marks) assumes you can read fluently.
Letters Ъ and Ь are never pronounced on their own. Ы never starts a native word. Ё is usually printed as Е but is always stressed when it appears.

units.title: 2. Hard vs. soft vowels
units.summary: Russian vowels come in pairs that signal whether the preceding consonant is hard or soft — the backbone of Russian pronunciation and spelling.

blocks.html: Each of Russia's vowel sounds has two letters: one marks a preceding hard consonant, the other a soft (palatalised) one. At the start of a word or after a vowel, the soft-series letters add a /j/ ("y") glide.

units.title: 3. Stress & vowel reduction
units.summary: Russian stress is free and mobile, and unstressed vowels reduce. This single rule explains most of the gap between spelling and speech.

blocks.html: Every Russian word has exactly one stressed syllable, pronounced longer and clearer. Stress is free (it can fall anywhere) and mobile (it can move between forms of the same word: рука́ → ру́ки). Dictionaries mark it with an acute accent; ordinary text does not.

items: Unstressed О is pronounced like /a/: молоко́ sounds like 'malako'.
Unstressed Е and Я reduce toward /i/: язы́к sounds like 'yizyk'.
Getting stress wrong is the #1 marker of a foreign accent — learn each word's stress with the word itself.

items.en: good/well — only the last О is full
castle vs. lock — stress changes meaning

units.title: 4. Consonant assimilation & devoicing
units.summary: Voiced consonants devoice at the end of words and assimilate to their neighbours.

items: Final devoicing: voiced consonants become voiceless at the end of a word — хлеб sounds like 'khlep', город like 'gorot'.
Regressive assimilation: a consonant takes the voicing of the one after it — водка sounds like 'votka', сделать like 'zdelat'.
Spelling stays constant even though the sound changes — this is why reading aloud needs the rules above.

blocks.html: Voiced/voiceless pairs: Б/П, В/Ф, Г/К, Д/Т, Ж/Ш, З/С. Always voiceless: Х, Ц, Ч, Щ. Always voiced (sonorant): Л, М, Н, Р, Й.
